text: Philosopher Martin Heidegger described 'ready-to-hand' experience as the state in which a tool disappears from consciousness during skillful use: the hammer vanishes when you hammer well. This transparency is the phenomenology of flow. The writer who is truly absorbed does not feel the keyboard; the surgeon does not feel the scalpel. The moment a tool becomes 'present-at-hand' — noticed, analyzed, awkward — absorption breaks. Distraction, on this reading, is not merely an interruption of attention but an ontological shift: you are thrown back from absorbed doing into self-conscious observing, which is an entirely different relationship with the world.
question: According to the passage, what does Heidegger mean when a tool becomes 'present-at-hand'?

options.text: The tool is being used for the first time and requires careful study.
The tool is functioning perfectly and no longer requires conscious monitoring.
The tool becomes noticed and analyzed, breaking absorbed, skillful engagement.
The tool has been mastered and can now be used in a flow state automatically.
